section.title: Grad, curl, and divergence in common coordinates

subsection.title: Cartesian coordinates
subsection.p: That is, \boxed{\nabla\times\mathbf F=(R_y-Q_z)\mathbf i+(P_z-R_x)\mathbf j+(Q_x-P_y)\mathbf k.}

subsection.title: Plane Cartesian formulas
subsection.p: For \mathbf F=(P,Q), the scalar curl in the plane is \boxed{\operatorname{curl}_{\mathrm{plane}}\mathbf F=Q_x-P_y.}
The associated exterior derivative is \boxed{d(P\,dx+Q\,dy)=(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy.}

subsection.title: Cylindrical coordinates
subsection.p: Gradient: \boxed{\nabla f=f_r\,\widehat{\mathbf e}_r+\frac1r f_\theta\,\widehat{\mathbf e}_\theta+f_z\,\widehat{\mathbf e}_z.}
Divergence: \boxed{\nabla\cdot\mathbf F=\frac1r\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(rF_r)+\frac1r\frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial\theta}+\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}.}

subsection.title: Spherical coordinates
subsection.p:
Curl:
\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \nabla\times\mathbf F \amp= \frac1{\rho\sin\phi} \left( \frac{\partial}{\partial\phi}(\sin\phi\,F_\theta) - \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial\theta} \right) \widehat{\mathbf e}_\rho\\[1mm] \amp\quad+ \frac1\rho \left( \frac1{\sin\phi}\frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial\theta} - \frac{\partial}{\partial\rho}(\rho F_\theta) \right) \widehat{\mathbf e}_\phi\\[1mm] \amp\quad+ \frac1\rho \left( \frac{\partial}{\partial\rho}(\rho F_\phi) - \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial\phi} \right) \widehat{\mathbf e}_\theta. \end{aligned} }

warning.p: In polar, cylindrical, and spherical formulas, F_r,\ F_\theta,\ F_\rho,\ F_\phi,\ F_z are components in the local unit-vector basis, not Cartesian components.
For example, \mathbf F=F_r\widehat{\mathbf e}_r+F_\theta\widehat{\mathbf e}_\theta does not mean \mathbf F=(F_r,F_\theta) in Cartesian coordinates.
Polar and cylindrical formulas with 1/r require r\gt 0.
Coordinate singularities do not always mean the field itself is singular. They may mean the coordinate system has broken down.
